title: Performance Review: Supporting Development
learning_objective: Practice a coaching approach to performance reviews, using reflections to support colleague development while addressing performance concerns
scenario_context: A colleague is in a performance review discussion. They may feel defensive, uncertain about their performance, or resistant to feedback. Your role is to use a facilitative coaching approach - helping them reflect on their performance, identify areas for development, and commit to their own improvement goals. This applies core MI skills to internal colleague contexts.

persona_config.name: Sam
persona_config.background: Has been in the role for 18 months. Performance has been inconsistent - some good work but also missed deadlines and quality issues. Feeling defensive about the review process.

node_1.persona_text: I've been dreading this meeting, if I'm honest. Performance reviews always feel like I'm going to get told what I'm doing wrong.

choice_points.option_text: Well, performance reviews aren't just about what's wrong - they're about development and growth too.

feedback.immediate: This is well-intentioned reassurance, but you haven't reflected their anxiety. You've jumped to correcting their view instead of understanding it.
feedback.learning_note: In performance conversations, reflecting anxiety first helps colleagues feel heard before you reframe.

choice_points.option_text: You've been dreading this meeting because you're worried it's just going to be about what's doing wrong.

feedback.immediate: Excellent reflection! You've captured their anxiety and their expectation of criticism. This helps them feel understood rather than defended.
feedback.learning_note: Reflecting concerns about performance reviews reduces defensiveness and opens up productive conversation.

choice_points.option_text: What is it about performance reviews that makes you feel that way?
choice_points.preview_hint: This question explores but may feel intrusive

feedback.immediate: This is an open question, but after expressing vulnerability, they might benefit from having their feelings reflected first.
feedback.learning_note: Questions about anxiety can work, but reflection often feels safer in vulnerable moments.

node_2a.persona_text: I suppose. I know I've had some issues this year. But it's not like I don't try, you know?

choice_points.option_text: I can see you've been trying, but there have been some problems with deadlines and quality that we need to address.
choice_points.preview_hint: This acknowledges but pivots to criticism

feedback.immediate: You've briefly acknowledged their effort but quickly moved to criticism. This can make colleagues feel their efforts aren't truly seen.
feedback.learning_note: In performance conversations, fully reflecting effort before addressing concerns builds more trust.

choice_points.option_text: You feel like you do try, and you want that to be recognized alongside the issues.

feedback.immediate: Excellent double-sided reflection! You've captured their effort AND their concern about it being overlooked. This builds trust for the harder conversation ahead.
feedback.learning_note: Double-sided reflections in performance reviews acknowledge both strengths AND development areas without either side feeling dismissed.

node_2b.persona_text: Exactly. Because when I do good work, nobody seems to notice. But make one mistake and everyone's jumping on it.

choice_points.option_text: I think that's an exaggeration - your manager gave you positive feedback on the Smith project just last month.

feedback.immediate: You've argued with their perception rather than understanding it. Even if you're factually correct, they'll feel unheard and become more defensive.
feedback.learning_note: In performance conversations, acknowledge perceptions before correcting them. 'You feel like...' creates safety.

choice_points.option_text: It feels like your good work goes unnoticed, but mistakes get a lot of attention.
choice_points.preview_hint: This reflects their perception without arguing

feedback.immediate: Good reflection! By not arguing with their perception, you've shown you're willing to understand their experience. This opens the door for a more balanced discussion later.
feedback.learning_note: Reflecting unfairness perceptions doesn't mean agreeing with them - it means acknowledging they feel that way.

node_2c.persona_text: I don't know... past experiences, I guess. Previous managers weren't great at giving feedback.

choice_points.option_text: Well, I'm not your previous manager, and I want this to be a constructive conversation for you.

feedback.immediate: While true, this focuses on you rather than their experience. Acknowledging their past experience would help more before explaining your approach.
feedback.learning_note: In performance conversations, acknowledging past negative experiences builds trust before explaining your approach.

choice_points.option_text: Past experiences with performance reviews have left you feeling this way.

feedback.immediate: Excellent reflection. You've acknowledged their past experience without dismissing it. This helps them feel this conversation might be different.
feedback.learning_note: Reflecting past negative experiences in performance conversations helps colleagues feel safe to engage in the current one.

node_3a.persona_text: Right, but we need to focus on the issues. I'm here to listen and improve.

node_3b.persona_text: Yeah. Thanks for hearing that. It helps to know my effort is seen.

node_3c.persona_text: Maybe. But I still feel like there's a double standard.

node_3d.persona_text: Exactly. So when we talk about the issues... I need to know my effort counts too.

node_3e.persona_text: I appreciate that. Let's see how this goes then.

node_3f.persona_text: Yeah, thanks. That means a lot that you understand that.
